arch.title: Architecture Visualizer
arch.tooltip.comp-app-service: Application service that orchestrates use case execution and cross-cutting concerns
arch.tooltip.comp-cache-adapter: Infrastructure cache implementation for read optimization
arch.tooltip.comp-cl-entity: Enterprise-wide business rules at the innermost core, independent of everything
arch.tooltip.comp-command: Immutable data object representing an intent to change state
arch.tooltip.comp-cqrs-aggregate: Domain object enforcing business rules on the write side
arch.tooltip.comp-cqrs-cmdbus: Dispatches commands to appropriate handlers with middleware support
arch.tooltip.comp-cqrs-cmdhandler: Processes a command: loads aggregate, executes domain logic, stores events
arch.tooltip.comp-cqrs-command: Immutable object representing an intent to change state
arch.tooltip.comp-cqrs-eventbus: Routes events from write side to projection consumers for read model updates
arch.tooltip.comp-cqrs-eventstore: Append-only storage for domain events, the source of truth
arch.tooltip.comp-cqrs-query: Read-only request for data that does not modify state
arch.tooltip.comp-cqrs-querybus: Routes queries to specialized handlers with middleware
arch.tooltip.comp-cqrs-queryhandler: Reads from optimized read model, never modifies write-side state
arch.tooltip.comp-cqrs-readdb: Database optimized for read queries, separate from write store
arch.tooltip.comp-cqrs-readmodel: Denormalized projection optimized for specific query patterns
arch.tooltip.comp-db-adapter: Translates domain operations into database queries
arch.tooltip.comp-ddd-acl: Translation layer protecting context boundaries from foreign model concepts
arch.tooltip.comp-ddd-aggregate: Cluster of domain objects treated as a transactional consistency boundary
arch.tooltip.comp-ddd-appservice: Coordinates use case execution without containing business logic
arch.tooltip.comp-ddd-controller: Presentation entry point receiving requests into the Bounded Context
arch.tooltip.comp-ddd-domservice: Stateless logic spanning multiple aggregates or entities
arch.tooltip.comp-ddd-dto: Data Transfer Object for cross-layer communication
arch.tooltip.comp-ddd-entity: Domain object with unique identity and lifecycle
arch.tooltip.comp-ddd-event: Record of something significant that happened in the domain
arch.tooltip.comp-ddd-eventbus: Infrastructure for publishing domain events across contexts
arch.tooltip.comp-ddd-pay-entity: Payment entity within the Payment Bounded Context
arch.tooltip.comp-ddd-pay-handler: Receives and processes domain events from other Bounded Contexts
arch.tooltip.comp-ddd-pay-service: Domain service handling payment processing logic
arch.tooltip.comp-ddd-repo: Persistence abstraction for storing and retrieving aggregates
arch.tooltip.comp-ddd-repo-iface: Domain interface defining persistence contract — implemented in Infrastructure
arch.tooltip.comp-ddd-spec: Encapsulates a business rule as a reusable boolean predicate
arch.tooltip.comp-ddd-usecase: Application layer orchestrator for domain operations
arch.tooltip.comp-ddd-vo: Immutable object defined by its attributes, encapsulates validation
arch.tooltip.comp-de-event: Domain event raised after order is placed
arch.tooltip.comp-de-eventbus: Event Bus distributes domain events to all subscribed contexts
arch.tooltip.comp-de-place: Aggregate method creates order and raises domain event
arch.tooltip.comp-de-publisher: Infrastructure adapter publishes domain event to external bus
arch.tooltip.comp-domain-event: Record of something significant that happened in the domain
arch.tooltip.comp-domain-service: Stateless domain service coordinating logic across multiple entities
arch.tooltip.comp-eda-bus: Messaging infrastructure routing events from producers to interested consumers
arch.tooltip.comp-eda-cons-analytics: Tracks metrics and builds reports from all domain events
arch.tooltip.comp-eda-cons-inventory: Reacts to order events by reserving or releasing stock items
arch.tooltip.comp-eda-cons-notify: Sends notifications (email, SMS, push) in response to domain events
arch.tooltip.comp-eda-cons-payment: Processes payments in response to order creation events
arch.tooltip.comp-eda-cons-shipping: Creates shipments when orders are confirmed and paid
arch.tooltip.comp-eda-prod-order: Publishes order-related domain events (OrderCreated, OrderConfirmed, etc.)
arch.tooltip.comp-eda-saga: Central coordinator managing distributed transaction steps and compensations
arch.tooltip.comp-entity: Core business object with unique identity and business rules
arch.tooltip.comp-entry: Entry point that receives requests, maps input to DTO, and delegates to UseCase
arch.tooltip.comp-es-aggregate-apply: State rebuilt, applies new command and emits a new domain event
arch.tooltip.comp-es-aggregate-load: Loads aggregate state by replaying events from the Event Store
arch.tooltip.comp-es-command: Intent to change state, triggers event emission from the aggregate
arch.tooltip.comp-es-eventbus: Publishes committed events to downstream consumers asynchronously
arch.tooltip.comp-es-evt1: Immutable event: order was created with initial data
arch.tooltip.comp-es-evt2: Immutable event: an item was added to the order
arch.tooltip.comp-es-evt3: Immutable event: another item was added to the order
arch.tooltip.comp-es-evt4: Immutable event: order was confirmed by the customer
arch.tooltip.comp-es-evt5: Immutable event: payment was received for the order
arch.tooltip.comp-es-handler: Routes command to the appropriate aggregate for processing
arch.tooltip.comp-es-new: Next event to be appended to the stream
arch.tooltip.comp-es-proj-detail: Read model: detailed order view with all items and status history
arch.tooltip.comp-es-proj-list: Read model: flat list of orders optimized for listing and filtering
arch.tooltip.comp-es-proj-search: Read model: full-text search index for fast order lookup
arch.tooltip.comp-es-proj-stats: Read model: aggregated statistics like revenue, counts, averages
arch.tooltip.comp-event-dispatcher: Infrastructure implementation delivering domain events to subscribers
arch.tooltip.comp-event-dispatcher-inter: Interface for publishing domain events, defined in Domain layer
arch.tooltip.comp-hex-api: Implements output port for calling external services
arch.tooltip.comp-hex-cache: Implements output port for cache read/write operations
arch.tooltip.comp-hex-cli: Converts CLI commands into port method invocations
arch.tooltip.comp-hex-db: Implements output port for database persistence
arch.tooltip.comp-hex-email: Implements output port for sending emails and notifications
arch.tooltip.comp-hex-entity: Core business object with identity and business rules
arch.tooltip.comp-hex-http: Receives HTTP requests and translates them into port calls
arch.tooltip.comp-hex-input-port: Interface for incoming requests. Driving adapters depend on this port.
arch.tooltip.comp-hex-msg: Consumes messages from queue and calls input ports
arch.tooltip.comp-hex-output-port: Interface for outgoing operations. Driven adapters implement this port.
arch.tooltip.comp-hex-queue: Implements output port for publishing messages to queue
arch.tooltip.comp-hex-service: Domain logic coordination. Depends on: Entity, Output Port (I)
arch.tooltip.comp-hex-usecase: Application layer: orchestrates the use case, calls domain services and entities
arch.tooltip.comp-ms-client: External client application (web, mobile, or API consumer).
arch.tooltip.comp-ms-gateway: Single entry point for all client requests. Handles routing, authentication, rate limiting, and response aggregation.
arch.tooltip.comp-ms-inventory-svc: Manages product stock levels, reservations, and warehouse operations. Owns the inventory database.
arch.tooltip.comp-ms-notification-svc: Sends emails, SMS, and push notifications. Consumes events from the Message Queue — never called directly.
arch.tooltip.comp-ms-orchestrator: Central coordinator that manages the saga workflow. Sends commands to services, waits for replies, and triggers compensating transactions on failure.
arch.tooltip.comp-ms-order-svc: Handles order creation, status tracking, and order lifecycle. Orchestrates calls to Inventory and Payment via Gateway.
arch.tooltip.comp-ms-payment-svc: Processes payments, manages transactions, and handles refunds. Owns the payments database.
arch.tooltip.comp-ms-user-svc: Manages user accounts, authentication, and profile data. Owns its own user database.
arch.tooltip.comp-mvc-data: Application state and data storage, notifies observers on change
arch.tooltip.comp-mvc-handler: Interprets user input and invokes corresponding Model methods
arch.tooltip.comp-mvc-logic: Business rules and validation logic, independent of UI
arch.tooltip.comp-mvc-render: Observes Model changes and triggers re-rendering of the UI
arch.tooltip.comp-mvc-router: Routes incoming requests to the appropriate Controller action
arch.tooltip.comp-mvc-template: HTML template that displays Model data to the user
arch.tooltip.comp-mvp-data: Business data storage, no knowledge of Presenter or View
arch.tooltip.comp-mvp-format: Transforms raw Model data into display-friendly format
arch.tooltip.comp-mvp-iface: Interface contract allowing Presenter to update View without knowing its type
arch.tooltip.comp-mvp-logic: Business rules and validation, accessed only through Presenter
arch.tooltip.comp-mvp-preslogic: Contains all presentation logic: reads Model, formats, pushes to View
arch.tooltip.comp-mvp-ui: Passive UI elements with zero logic, only renders what Presenter says
arch.tooltip.comp-mvvm-binding: Two-way binding mechanism keeping View and ViewModel in sync
arch.tooltip.comp-mvvm-commands: Encapsulate user actions with execute and can-execute logic
arch.tooltip.comp-mvvm-data: Pure data layer, no knowledge of View or ViewModel
arch.tooltip.comp-mvvm-logic: Business rules and validation, accessed by ViewModel
arch.tooltip.comp-mvvm-observable: Properties that notify subscribers when values change, triggering UI updates
arch.tooltip.comp-mvvm-ui: Declarative UI that binds to ViewModel properties, minimal code-behind
arch.tooltip.comp-ohs-event: Domain event raised when order is placed
arch.tooltip.comp-ohs-publisher: Infrastructure adapter publishing events through OHS contract
arch.tooltip.comp-ohs-service: Open Host Service exposes a formal API with Published Language (OpenAPI/Protobuf schema)
arch.tooltip.comp-repo-impl: Concrete persistence implementation of the Repository interface
arch.tooltip.comp-repo-interface: Interface abstracting persistence, defined in Domain layer
arch.tooltip.comp-request-dto: Typed data object mapping raw input into structured request data
arch.tooltip.comp-response-dto: Structured output data formatted for the client response
arch.tooltip.comp-saga-eventbus: Event Bus carries commands from Saga to contexts and events back to Saga
arch.tooltip.comp-saga-orchestrator: Saga Orchestrator coordinates the cross-context business process and manages compensating actions
arch.tooltip.comp-sagaf-eventbus: Event Bus carries commands and events between Saga and contexts
arch.tooltip.comp-sagaf-orchestrator: Saga Orchestrator detects failure and triggers compensating transactions in reverse order
arch.tooltip.comp-usecase: Application orchestrator that coordinates domain services and persistence
arch.tooltip.comp-value-object: Immutable object defined by attributes, encapsulates validation and equality
arch.tooltip.rel-acl: Payment protects itself from Order model changes via ACL
arch.tooltip.rel-conformist: CRM conforms to Order model without any translation
arch.tooltip.rel-shared-kernel: ProductId jointly owned by Order and Inventory teams
mvc.name: MVC / MVP / MVVM
mvc.modes.mvc.label: MVC
mvc.modes.mvc.desc: Model-View-Controller: Controller receives user input, updates the Model, and the View observes Model changes. The Controller does NOT update the View directly - the View pulls data from the Model.
mvc.modes.mvp.label: MVP
mvc.modes.mvp.desc: Model-View-Presenter: The Presenter acts as a mediator. View delegates all user input to the Presenter. Presenter updates the Model and explicitly updates the View. View is passive (no logic).
mvc.modes.mvvm.label: MVVM
mvc.modes.mvvm.desc: Model-View-ViewModel: ViewModel exposes data and commands via data binding. View binds to ViewModel properties. When ViewModel changes, View updates automatically. Two-way binding keeps them in sync.
mvc.details.mvc.principles: Separation of Concerns: each component has a single responsibility
Observer Pattern: View subscribes to Model changes, not pushed by Controller
Controller never updates View directly — only selects View and updates Model
Model is completely independent of UI (no references to View or Controller)
Multiple Views can observe the same Model simultaneously

mvc.details.mvc.concepts.definition: Encapsulates application data and business rules. Notifies observers when state changes.
Renders Model data to the user. Observes Model for changes. Forwards user input to Controller.
Interprets user input, invokes Model methods, selects appropriate View.
Design pattern where Model maintains a list of dependents (Views) and notifies them of state changes.

mvc.details.mvc.tradeoffs.pros: Simple and intuitive — easy to understand and implement
Widely supported by web frameworks (Rails, Django, Spring MVC, Laravel)
Good fit for UI-driven applications with clear data-display separation
Extensive ecosystem of tools, tutorials, and community support
mvc.details.mvc.tradeoffs.cons: Fat controllers — business logic tends to accumulate in controllers
Model ambiguity — "Model" can mean domain object, DTO, or database row
Does not scale well for complex domains with many interrelated rules
View-Model coupling through observer pattern can create hidden dependencies
mvc.details.mvc.tradeoffs.whenToUse: Best for web applications with small-to-medium complexity, framework-driven development, and teams that need a well-known, low-friction architecture pattern.
mvc.details.mvp.principles: Passive View: View contains zero presentation logic, only UI rendering
Presenter is the sole mediator between View and Model
View communicates with Presenter through a well-defined interface (IView)
Presenter explicitly updates View — no observer pattern, no data binding
High testability: Presenter can be unit-tested with a mock View interface

mvc.details.mvp.concepts.definition: View pattern where the View has no logic and delegates all decisions to the Presenter.
Contains all presentation logic. Reads Model, formats data, and explicitly pushes updates to View.
Contract that View implements, allowing Presenter to update UI without knowing the concrete View.
MVP variant where View handles simple data binding, and Presenter handles complex logic only.

mvc.details.mvp.tradeoffs.pros: Highly testable — Presenter can be unit-tested with a mock View interface
Passive View eliminates UI logic bugs
Clear separation of presentation logic from display
Well-suited for desktop and mobile applications
mvc.details.mvp.tradeoffs.cons: More boilerplate — every View needs an interface and a Presenter
Presenter can become bloated with too much presentation logic
One-to-one View-Presenter mapping can be rigid
Less framework support compared to MVC
mvc.details.mvp.tradeoffs.whenToUse: Best for applications requiring high testability of presentation logic, desktop/mobile apps with complex UI interactions, and teams that want explicit control over View updates.
mvc.details.mvvm.principles: Two-way Data Binding: View and ViewModel stay in sync automatically
ViewModel has no direct reference to View — communicates through bindings only
Command Pattern: user actions are bound to ViewModel commands, not event handlers
ViewModel transforms Model data into View-friendly format (presentation logic)
Declarative UI: View declares bindings in markup, not imperative code

mvc.details.mvvm.concepts.definition: Mechanism that connects View properties to ViewModel properties, syncing changes in both directions.
Abstraction of the View that exposes observable properties and commands. Acts as data context.
Property that notifies subscribers (bindings) when its value changes, triggering UI updates.
Object that encapsulates an action and its "can execute" logic, bound to UI elements like buttons.

mvc.details.mvvm.tradeoffs.pros: Declarative UI with automatic synchronization via data binding
ViewModel is highly testable without any UI framework dependency
Reduces boilerplate compared to MVP — no explicit View update calls
Excellent framework support (WPF, SwiftUI, Vue.js, Angular)
mvc.details.mvvm.tradeoffs.cons: Data binding can be hard to debug when things go wrong
Memory leaks from forgotten subscriptions to observables
Overhead of observable wrappers for every property
Two-way binding can create unexpected cascading updates
mvc.details.mvvm.tradeoffs.whenToUse: Best for rich client applications with reactive UIs, frameworks that support data binding natively, and scenarios where UI state management is a primary concern.
layered.name: Layered Architecture
layered.modes.http.label: HTTP Request
layered.modes.http.desc: HTTP request enters through Controller in Presentation layer. Controller passes Request DTO to UseCase. UseCase creates a Command, delegates to AppService and domain services. After persistence, UseCase dispatches domain events through EventDispatcher interface (Domain), resolved to implementation in Infrastructure.
layered.modes.console.label: Console Command
layered.modes.console.desc: Console command enters through CLI handler, delegates to AppService which orchestrates UseCase directly (no CommandBus). Infrastructure handles persistence. Dependencies always point inward.
layered.modes.message.label: Message Consumer
layered.modes.message.desc: Async message enters through a message consumer, AppService handles orchestration, UseCase executes domain logic. After persistence, UseCase dispatches domain events through EventDispatcher interface in Domain layer.
layered.details.http.principles: Strict top-down dependency: each layer depends only on the layer directly below it
Separation of Concerns: Presentation handles I/O, Application orchestrates, Domain owns rules, Infrastructure persists
Domain Layer is independent: no references to frameworks, databases, or transport
Dependency Inversion: Domain defines interfaces (Repository, EventDispatcher), Infrastructure implements them
Each layer can be replaced independently without affecting the others

layered.details.http.concepts.definition: Entry point in the Presentation layer. Receives HTTP request, maps input to DTO, delegates to UseCase, returns Response.
Application layer orchestrator. Receives a DTO, creates a Command, coordinates domain services and persistence.
Immutable data object representing an intent to change state. Created by UseCase from the incoming DTO.
Record of something that happened in the domain. Dispatched after persistence through the EventDispatcher interface.
Interface defined in Domain layer. Abstracts persistence so domain logic never depends on storage details.

layered.details.http.tradeoffs.pros: Simple and well-understood — most developers know layered architecture
Clear separation of concerns between presentation, application, domain, and infrastructure
Easy to learn and onboard new team members
Good tooling and framework support across languages
layered.details.http.tradeoffs.cons: Tight coupling between layers — changes often cascade through multiple layers
Does not scale well for complex domains with many cross-cutting concerns
Business logic can leak into controllers or repositories over time
Tendency toward "big ball of mud" without discipline
layered.details.http.tradeoffs.whenToUse: Best for simple applications, CRUD-heavy systems, small teams, and learning projects where the overhead of more sophisticated architectures is not justified.
layered.details.console.principles: Strict top-down dependency: each layer depends only on the layer directly below it
Separation of Concerns: Presentation handles I/O, Application orchestrates, Domain owns rules, Infrastructure persists
Domain Layer is independent: no references to frameworks, databases, or transport
Console and HTTP share the same Application and Domain layers — only Presentation differs
Value Objects enforce business constraints at the domain boundary, not in the UI layer

layered.details.console.concepts.definition: Presentation layer entry point for CLI. Parses arguments, builds DTO, delegates to AppService.
Application layer service that orchestrates UseCase execution and cross-cutting concerns.
Immutable domain object defined by its attributes. Encapsulates validation and equality logic.
Infrastructure component that implements caching. Domain and Application layers never reference it directly.

layered.details.console.tradeoffs.pros: Simple and well-understood — most developers know layered architecture
Clear separation of concerns between presentation, application, domain, and infrastructure
Easy to learn and onboard new team members
Good tooling and framework support across languages
layered.details.console.tradeoffs.cons: Tight coupling between layers — changes often cascade through multiple layers
Does not scale well for complex domains with many cross-cutting concerns
Business logic can leak into controllers or repositories over time
Tendency toward "big ball of mud" without discipline
layered.details.console.tradeoffs.whenTo: Best for simple applications, CRUD-heavy systems, small teams, and learning projects where the overhead of more sophisticated architectures is not justified.
layered.details.message.principles: Strict top-down dependency: each layer depends only on the layer directly below it
Separation of Concerns: Presentation handles I/O, Application orchestrates, Domain owns rules, Infrastructure persists
Domain Layer is independent: no references to frameworks, databases, or transport
Asynchronous entry points follow the same layered flow as synchronous ones
Domain events are dispatched through an interface defined in the Domain layer, resolved in Infrastructure

layered.details.message.concepts.definition: Presentation layer handler for async messages. Deserializes payload into a DTO and delegates to AppService.
Stateless service in the Domain layer that coordinates logic spanning multiple Entities or Value Objects.
Interface in the Domain layer for publishing domain events. Infrastructure provides the concrete implementation.
Infrastructure implementation that delivers domain events to subscribers (queue, bus, or in-process handlers).

layered.details.message.tradeoffs.pros: Simple and well-understood — most developers know layered architecture
Clear separation of concerns between presentation, application, domain, and infrastructure
Easy to learn and onboard new team members
Good tooling and framework support across languages
layered.details.message.tradeoffs.cons: Tight coupling between layers — changes often cascade through multiple layers
Does not scale well for complex domains with many cross-cutting concerns
Business logic can leak into controllers or repositories over time
Tendency toward "big ball of mud" without discipline
layered.details.message.tradeoffs.whenTo: Best for simple applications, CRUD-heavy systems, small teams, and learning projects where the overhead of more sophisticated architectures is not justified.
clean.name: Clean Architecture
clean.modes.http.label: HTTP Request
clean.modes.http.desc: Request flows inward through concentric rings: Frameworks & Drivers → Interface Adapters → Use Cases → Entities. The response flows back outward through OutputBoundary and Presenter. Dependencies always point inward — the Dependency Rule is the overriding principle.
clean.modes.console.label: Console Command
clean.modes.console.desc: Console command enters through the outermost ring (Frameworks) and flows inward through adapters, InputBoundary, and use cases to entities. The same Dependency Rule applies — inner layers never reference outer layers.
clean.modes.message.label: Message Consumer
clean.modes.message.desc: Async message arrives at the Frameworks ring, flows through Interface Adapters and InputBoundary to Use Cases and Entities. Output returns via OutputBoundary and Presenter through the outer rings.
clean.details.http.principles: The Dependency Rule: source code dependencies must point only inward — inner rings never reference outer rings
Entities encapsulate enterprise-wide business rules, independent of any application or framework
Use Cases contain application-specific business rules and orchestrate data flow to and from Entities
Interface Adapters convert data between the format used by Use Cases/Entities and the format needed by external agents (DB, Web, UI)
Frameworks & Drivers are the outermost ring — they are kept as thin as possible and serve as glue code
Boundaries are crossed via interfaces (InputBoundary, OutputBoundary) so inner layers stay decoupled from outer layers

clean.details.http.concepts.definition: Interface defined in the Use Cases ring. Controllers call this interface to invoke a use case, ensuring the adapter layer never leaks into business logic.
Interface defined in the Use Cases ring. UseCase calls this to pass results outward, so it never depends on Presenter or UI details.
Interface Adapter that implements OutputBoundary. Converts use case output into a ViewModel suitable for the view layer.
Interface Adapter implementing repository interfaces defined by Use Cases. Translates between domain objects and database operations.
Plain data structure created by the Presenter. Contains only display-ready data — no business logic, no framework dependencies.

clean.details.http.tradeoffs.pros: Highly testable — business logic is isolated from frameworks and infrastructure
Framework-independent — swap frameworks without touching domain or use cases
Domain-focused — entities and use cases are at the center, not infrastructure
Clear dependency rules make it easy to reason about code boundaries
clean.details.http.tradeoffs.cons: More code and abstractions (boundaries, presenters, gateways) than simpler architectures
Mapping overhead between layers (DTOs, ViewModels, domain objects)
Can feel over-engineered for simple CRUD applications
Steeper onboarding curve for developers unfamiliar with the pattern
clean.details.http.tradeoffs.whenToUse: Best for long-lived projects where framework independence matters, complex domains requiring thorough testing, and systems where you expect the delivery mechanism or infrastructure to change over time.
clean.details.console.principles: The Dependency Rule: source code dependencies must point only inward — inner rings never reference outer rings
Console and HTTP share the same Use Cases and Entities — only the outer rings differ
The CLI Framework is a detail in the outermost ring, easily replaceable without touching business logic
InputBoundary ensures that the Controller (adapter) never couples to the concrete UseCase implementation
OutputBoundary allows the UseCase to return results without knowing whether output goes to a terminal, web page, or file

clean.details.console.concepts.definition: Outermost ring component (Frameworks & Drivers). Provides argument parsing, command routing, and I/O — treated as a replaceable detail.
Interface Adapter that translates CLI arguments into a request DTO and calls InputBoundary. Decouples the framework from application logic.
Application-specific business rule orchestrator. Receives input via InputBoundary, interacts with Entities, and returns results via OutputBoundary.
Enterprise-wide business object at the core. Contains critical business rules that are the least likely to change when something external changes.

clean.details.console.tradeoffs.pros: Highly testable — business logic is isolated from frameworks and infrastructure
Framework-independent — swap frameworks without touching domain or use cases
Domain-focused — entities and use cases are at the center, not infrastructure
Clear dependency rules make it easy to reason about code boundaries
clean.details.console.tradeoffs.cons: More code and abstractions (boundaries, presenters, gateways) than simpler architectures
Mapping overhead between layers (DTOs, ViewModels, domain objects)
Can feel over-engineered for simple CRUD applications
Steeper onboarding curve for developers unfamiliar with the pattern
clean.details.console.tradeoffs.whenToUs: Best for long-lived projects where framework independence matters, complex domains requiring thorough testing, and systems where you expect the delivery mechanism or infrastructure to change over time.
clean.details.message.principles: The Dependency Rule: source code dependencies must point only inward — inner rings never reference outer rings
Async message processing follows the same ring structure as synchronous requests — transport is a detail
The Queue Driver is a framework detail in the outermost ring, interchangeable without modifying business logic
Boundaries (InputBoundary, OutputBoundary) ensure that messaging infrastructure never leaks into Use Cases or Entities
Entities remain pure regardless of whether they are invoked by HTTP, CLI, or message — that is the power of the Dependency Rule

clean.details.message.concepts.definition: Outermost ring component that receives messages from a broker (RabbitMQ, Kafka, SQS). A framework detail, easily swappable.
Interface in the Use Cases ring. The message Controller calls this to trigger use case logic, keeping the queue driver decoupled.
Interface in the Use Cases ring. UseCase pushes results outward through this boundary — the Presenter decides how to format the acknowledgment.
Interface Adapter that formats the use case output into an acknowledgment or event payload, without the UseCase knowing the delivery mechanism.

clean.details.message.tradeoffs.pros: Highly testable — business logic is isolated from frameworks and infrastructure
Framework-independent — swap frameworks without touching domain or use cases
Domain-focused — entities and use cases are at the center, not infrastructure
Clear dependency rules make it easy to reason about code boundaries
clean.details.message.tradeoffs.cons: More code and abstractions (boundaries, presenters, gateways) than simpler architectures
Mapping overhead between layers (DTOs, ViewModels, domain objects)
Can feel over-engineered for simple CRUD applications
Steeper onboarding curve for developers unfamiliar with the pattern
clean.details.message.tradeoffs.whenToUs: Best for long-lived projects where framework independence matters, complex domains requiring thorough testing, and systems where you expect the delivery mechanism or infrastructure to change over time.
hexagonal.name: Hexagonal (Ports & Adapters)
hexagonal.modes.http.label: HTTP Request
hexagonal.modes.http.desc: HTTP adapter (driving) receives request, calls input port, domain processes logic, output port delegates to driven adapter (database). Ports define the interface, adapters implement it.
hexagonal.modes.console.label: Console Command
hexagonal.modes.console.desc: CLI adapter (driving) triggers the same input port. The domain core is completely agnostic to whether it was triggered by HTTP, CLI, or message.
hexagonal.modes.message.label: Message Consumer
hexagonal.modes.message.desc: Message adapter (driving) consumes from queue. The hexagonal core processes identically - only the driving adapter differs.
hexagonal.details.http.principles: Ports define interfaces; adapters implement them
Domain core has zero knowledge of infrastructure
Driving adapters call input ports; driven adapters implement output ports
Symmetric: left side (driving) mirrors right side (driven)

hexagonal.details.http.concepts.definition: An interface that defines how the application communicates with the outside world
A concrete implementation of a port for a specific technology (HTTP, CLI, DB)
Application layer component that implements Input Port and orchestrates domain objects to fulfill a business scenario
Primary/left-side adapters that initiate interaction with the application
Secondary/right-side adapters that the application uses to interact with external systems

hexagonal.details.http.tradeoffs.pros: Easy to swap adapters (e.g., switch DB without touching domain)
Naturally testable — mock ports for unit tests
Clear separation between business logic and infrastructure
hexagonal.details.http.tradeoffs.cons: More interfaces and classes than simpler architectures
Can be overkill for CRUD-heavy applications
Mapping between port DTOs and domain objects adds boilerplate
hexagonal.details.http.tradeoffs.whenToU: Best for applications with complex business logic that must remain independent of delivery mechanisms and infrastructure. Ideal when you need to support multiple entry points (HTTP, CLI, queue) or plan to change infrastructure components.
hexagonal.details.console.principles: Same domain core, different driving adapter
CLI adapter transforms terminal input into port calls
Domain is completely agnostic to the trigger source

hexagonal.details.console.concepts.definition: A driving adapter that converts command-line arguments into port method calls
Any driving adapter can trigger the same use case — the hexagon does not change

hexagonal.details.console.tradeoffs.pros: Proves the domain is truly infrastructure-agnostic
Enables batch processing and cron jobs via CLI
Same business rules enforced regardless of entry point
hexagonal.details.console.tradeoffs.cons: Each new entry point requires a new adapter implementation
Input validation logic may need duplication across adapters
hexagonal.details.console.tradeoffs.when: When your application needs multiple entry points (web, CLI, queue consumer) that share the same business logic.
hexagonal.details.message.principles: Message consumer is just another driving adapter
Asynchronous processing with same domain guarantees
Output can publish to a queue via driven adapter

hexagonal.details.message.concepts.definition: A driving adapter that deserializes queue messages and calls input ports
A driven adapter that publishes events/messages to an external queue

hexagonal.details.message.tradeoffs.pros: Same domain logic for sync and async flows
hexagonal.details.message.tradeoffs.cons: Error handling and retries need careful design
hexagonal.details.message.tradeoffs.when: When you need asynchronous processing, event-driven communication between services, or want to decouple producers from consumers.
cqrs.name: CQRS
cqrs.modes.http.label: HTTP (Command)
cqrs.modes.http.desc: HTTP write request enters the Command Side: Command -> CommandBus -> CommandHandler -> Aggregate -> EventStore. Events are projected to the Read Model. Query Side serves reads from optimized read models.
cqrs.modes.console.label: Console (Query)
cqrs.modes.console.desc: Console query goes through the Query Side: Query -> QueryBus -> QueryHandler -> ReadModel -> Database. Read models are optimized for specific query patterns.
cqrs.modes.message.label: Message (Event)
cqrs.modes.message.desc: Domain event is consumed and triggers both a Command on the write side and a projection update on the read side. Shows how CQRS handles eventual consistency.
cqrs.details.http.principles: Command Side handles all writes through CommandBus and CommandHandlers
Events are the bridge between write and read sides
Read models are eventually consistent projections of write-side events
Write model is optimized for business rules; read model is optimized for queries

cqrs.details.http.concepts.definition: An immutable object representing an intent to change state. Dispatched via CommandBus to the appropriate handler.
Processes a specific command, loads the aggregate, executes domain logic, and stores resulting events.
Append-only storage for domain events. The source of truth for the write side.
A denormalized projection optimized for specific query patterns, rebuilt from events.

cqrs.details.http.tradeoffs.pros: Independent scaling of reads and writes
Read models optimized for specific query patterns
Clear separation between command and query responsibilities
cqrs.details.http.tradeoffs.cons: Increased system complexity and more moving parts
Eventual consistency between write and read models
More infrastructure to manage (event bus, projections, multiple databases)
cqrs.details.http.tradeoffs.whenToUse: Best for systems with high-read/low-write ratio, when read and write models have fundamentally different shapes, or when complex query patterns demand denormalized views.
cqrs.details.console.principles: Query Side serves reads from optimized, denormalized models
QueryBus routes queries to specialized handlers
Read models can be rebuilt entirely from the event stream
Different read models can serve different query patterns from the same events

cqrs.details.console.concepts.definition: A request for data that does not modify state. Routed via QueryBus to the appropriate handler.
Reads from the optimized read model and returns a result. Never modifies write-side state.
Dispatches queries to the correct handler. Enables middleware (caching, logging, authorization).

cqrs.details.console.tradeoffs.pros: Independent scaling of reads and writes
Read models optimized for specific query patterns
Clear separation between command and query responsibilities
cqrs.details.console.tradeoffs.cons: Increased system complexity and more moving parts
Eventual consistency between write and read models
More infrastructure to manage (event bus, projections, multiple databases)
cqrs.details.console.tradeoffs.whenToUse: Best for systems with high-read/low-write ratio, when read and write models have fundamentally different shapes, or when complex query patterns demand denormalized views.
cqrs.details.message.principles: Events from external systems can trigger commands on the write side
Projection updates happen asynchronously after events are stored
Eventual consistency is embraced as a design trade-off for scalability
The event bus decouples event producers from projection consumers

cqrs.details.message.concepts.definition: The process of transforming events into read model state. Can be replayed to rebuild read models.
Read models may lag behind the write model. The system guarantees convergence, not immediate consistency.

cqrs.details.message.tradeoffs.pros: Independent scaling of reads and writes
Read models optimized for specific query patterns
Clear separation between command and query responsibilities
cqrs.details.message.tradeoffs.cons: Increased system complexity and more moving parts
Eventual consistency between write and read models
More infrastructure to manage (event bus, projections, multiple databases)
cqrs.details.message.tradeoffs.whenToUse: Best for systems with high-read/low-write ratio, when read and write models have fundamentally different shapes, or when complex query patterns demand denormalized views.
eventsourcing.name: Event Sourcing
eventsourcing.modes.http.label: HTTP Request
eventsourcing.modes.http.desc: Command arrives via HTTP. Instead of updating state directly, the aggregate emits domain events. Events are stored in the Event Store as the single source of truth. State is rebuilt by replaying events.
eventsourcing.modes.console.label: Rebuild State
eventsourcing.modes.console.desc: Demonstrates state reconstruction: all events from the Event Store are replayed in order to rebuild the current aggregate state. This is the core advantage of Event Sourcing.
eventsourcing.modes.message.label: Projection Update
eventsourcing.modes.message.desc: Events from the Event Store are projected into read-optimized views (projections). Each projection can build a different representation of the same event stream.
eventsourcing.details.http.principles: Events are the single source of truth — state is derived, never stored directly
Aggregates are loaded by replaying their event stream
New state changes produce new events appended to the store
Projections transform events into query-optimized read models

eventsourcing.details.http.concepts.definition: An append-only log of domain events. The authoritative source of truth for all state changes.
The process of rebuilding aggregate state by sequentially applying all events from the stream.
A domain object whose state is rebuilt from events rather than loaded from a mutable row in a database.

eventsourcing.details.http.tradeoffs.pro: Complete audit trail — every state change is recorded as an immutable event
Time-travel and replay — reconstruct state at any point in history
Event-driven by nature — downstream systems react to events
No data loss — events are append-only, nothing is overwritten
eventsourcing.details.http.tradeoffs.con: Query complexity — cannot simply SELECT current state from a table
Schema evolution is hard — changing event structure affects replay
Storage growth — event streams grow indefinitely without snapshotting
Steep learning curve — fundamentally different from CRUD mental model
eventsourcing.details.http.tradeoffs.whe: Best for domains requiring a complete audit trail, the ability to replay or project events, and financial or compliance-driven systems where data loss is unacceptable.
eventsourcing.details.console.principles: State is never stored — it is always rebuilt from events
Replay applies events in order to reconstruct aggregate state
Snapshots can optimize replay for long event streams
The event stream is the single source of truth

eventsourcing.details.console.concepts.definition: Rebuilding the current state of an aggregate by replaying all its events from the beginning.
A periodic checkpoint of aggregate state to avoid replaying the entire event stream on every load.

Complete audit trail — every state change is recorded as an immutable event
Time-travel and replay — reconstruct state at any point in history
Event-driven by nature — downstream systems react to events
No data loss — events are append-only, nothing is overwritten
Query complexity — cannot simply SELECT current state from a table
Schema evolution is hard — changing event structure affects replay
Storage growth — event streams grow indefinitely without snapshotting
Steep learning curve — fundamentally different from CRUD mental model
Best for domains requiring a complete audit trail, the ability to replay or project events, and financial or compliance-driven systems where data loss is unacceptable.
eventsourcing.details.message.principles: Projections build different read representations from the same event stream
Each projection can focus on a specific query pattern or view
Projections are disposable — they can be deleted and rebuilt from events
Multiple projections can process the same events independently

eventsourcing.details.message.concepts.definition: A process that reads events and builds a read-optimized view (list, detail, statistics, search index).
A denormalized data structure built by a projection, optimized for a specific query pattern.

Complete audit trail — every state change is recorded as an immutable event
Time-travel and replay — reconstruct state at any point in history
Event-driven by nature — downstream systems react to events
No data loss — events are append-only, nothing is overwritten
Query complexity — cannot simply SELECT current state from a table
Schema evolution is hard — changing event structure affects replay
Storage growth — event streams grow indefinitely without snapshotting
Steep learning curve — fundamentally different from CRUD mental model
Best for domains requiring a complete audit trail, the ability to replay or project events, and financial or compliance-driven systems where data loss is unacceptable.
eda.name: Event-Driven Architecture
eda.modes.http.label: HTTP (Publish)
eda.modes.http.desc: HTTP request triggers an action that publishes a domain event to the Event Bus. Multiple consumers react independently. No direct coupling between producer and consumers.
eda.modes.choreography.label: Choreography
eda.modes.choreography.desc: Services react to events independently without central coordination. Each service listens for events it cares about and publishes new events. The flow emerges from individual service reactions.
eda.modes.orchestration.label: Orchestration (Saga)
eda.modes.orchestration.desc: A Saga orchestrator coordinates the distributed transaction. It sends commands to services and reacts to their responses, managing the overall workflow and compensation on failure.
eda.details.http.principles: Producers publish events without knowledge of consumers
Consumers react independently to events they subscribe to
Event Bus decouples producers from consumers completely
Multiple consumers can process the same event for different purposes

eda.details.http.concepts.definition: A messaging infrastructure that routes events from producers to interested consumers without direct coupling.
A service that publishes domain events when something significant happens in its domain.
A service that subscribes to events and reacts independently, without the producer knowing.

eda.details.http.tradeoffs.pros: Loose coupling — producers and consumers are completely independent
Scalability — add consumers without modifying producers
Resilience — failure in one consumer does not affect others
Natural fit for asynchronous workflows and integrations
eda.details.http.tradeoffs.cons: Eventual consistency — no immediate guarantee all consumers have processed
Debugging distributed event flows is significantly harder
Message ordering challenges across partitions and consumers
Harder to reason about the overall system behavior
eda.details.http.tradeoffs.whenToUse: Best when multiple independent consumers need to react to the same events, for async workflows, and when integrating loosely coupled systems or microservices.
eda.details.choreography.principles: No central coordinator — each service reacts and publishes independently
The overall flow emerges from individual service reactions
Each service owns its decision to react or not
New services can join the flow by subscribing to existing events

eda.details.choreography.concepts.definition: A coordination pattern where services react to events independently, with no central orchestrator controlling the flow.
A sequence of events where one service's reaction triggers events that other services react to, forming an implicit workflow.

eda.details.choreography.tradeoffs.pros: Loose coupling — producers and consumers are completely independent
Scalability — add consumers without modifying producers
Resilience — failure in one consumer does not affect others
Natural fit for asynchronous workflows and integrations
eda.details.choreography.tradeoffs.cons: Eventual consistency — no immediate guarantee all consumers have processed
Debugging distributed event flows is significantly harder
Message ordering challenges across partitions and consumers
Harder to reason about the overall system behavior
eda.details.choreography.tradeoffs.whenT: Best when multiple independent consumers need to react to the same events, for async workflows, and when integrating loosely coupled systems or microservices.
eda.details.orchestration.principles: Saga orchestrator manages the distributed transaction lifecycle
Each step sends a command and waits for a response event
Compensation logic handles failures at any step
The orchestrator is the single place to understand the full workflow

eda.details.orchestration.concepts.definition: A pattern for managing distributed transactions by coordinating a sequence of local transactions with compensating actions for rollback.
A central coordinator that sends commands to services and reacts to their response events, managing the overall workflow.
A rollback action executed when a step in the saga fails, undoing the effects of previously completed steps.

eda.details.orchestration.tradeoffs.pros: Loose coupling — producers and consumers are completely independent
Scalability — add consumers without modifying producers
Resilience — failure in one consumer does not affect others
Natural fit for asynchronous workflows and integrations
eda.details.orchestration.tradeoffs.cons: Eventual consistency — no immediate guarantee all consumers have processed
Debugging distributed event flows is significantly harder
Message ordering challenges across partitions and consumers
Harder to reason about the overall system behavior
eda.details.orchestration.tradeoffs.when: Best when multiple independent consumers need to react to the same events, for async workflows, and when integrating loosely coupled systems or microservices.
microservices.name: Microservices Architecture
microservices.modes.sync.label: Sync (REST/gRPC)
microservices.modes.sync.desc: Synchronous request flows through the API Gateway to individual services. The gateway authenticates, routes, and aggregates responses. Services communicate via REST or gRPC through the gateway — never directly.
microservices.modes.async.label: Async (Events)
microservices.modes.async.desc: Event-driven communication between services via a Message Queue. Services publish domain events after state changes. Other services consume relevant events and react independently. No direct service-to-service calls.
microservices.modes.saga.label: Saga ✓
microservices.modes.saga.desc: Successful distributed transaction coordinated by a Saga Orchestrator. Each step is a local transaction committed sequentially. All steps complete successfully and the order is fulfilled.
microservices.modes.saga-fail.label: Saga ✗
microservices.modes.saga-fail.desc: Failed saga with compensating transactions executed in reverse order. Payment fails after inventory is reserved, triggering compensations to restore consistency.
microservices.details.sync.principles: API Gateway is the single entry point — clients never call services directly
Each service owns its data store (Database per Service pattern)
Service Mesh is an infrastructure layer (sidecar proxies), not a separate service
All inter-service communication goes through the gateway — no direct service-to-service calls
Order Service orchestrates the workflow: calls Inventory, then Payment, then responds
Use timeouts, retries, and circuit breakers to handle failures gracefully

microservices.details.sync.concepts.definition: Reverse proxy that routes requests to appropriate services, handles authentication, rate limiting, and response aggregation.
Infrastructure layer (e.g., Istio, Linkerd) — sidecar proxies at each service managing discovery, load balancing, TLS, and observability. Not a separate node in the call chain.
Each microservice owns its private database. No direct database sharing between services.
Pattern that prevents cascading failures by detecting failures and short-circuiting calls to unhealthy services.

microservices.details.sync.tradeoffs.pro: Simple request-response model, easy to understand and debug
Strong consistency within a single request chain
Straightforward error handling with status codes
microservices.details.sync.tradeoffs.con: Temporal coupling — caller is blocked until all downstream services respond
Cascading failures if one service is slow or unavailable
Increased latency with each hop in the call chain
microservices.details.sync.tradeoffs.whe: Best for queries and operations that need immediate, consistent responses. Use when the client expects a synchronous result and the call chain is short (2-3 hops max).
microservices.details.async.principles: Services communicate via events through a Message Queue — no direct calls
Message Queue (Kafka/RabbitMQ) ensures reliable delivery and decouples producers from consumers
Notification Service is a pure event consumer — never called synchronously
Each service reacts to events it cares about independently
Eventual consistency — state converges over time, not immediately
Events are immutable facts describing what happened, not commands

microservices.details.async.concepts.definition: An immutable record of something that happened in a service (e.g., OrderCreated, PaymentProcessed).
Infrastructure (e.g., Kafka, RabbitMQ) that routes events from producers to interested consumers asynchronously.
Data across services will become consistent over time as events are processed, but may be temporarily inconsistent.
Consumers must handle duplicate events safely. Processing the same event twice should produce the same result.

microservices.details.async.tradeoffs.pr: Loose coupling — services are fully independent
High resilience — failures in one service do not cascade
Better scalability — services process events at their own pace
Natural audit trail from event streams
microservices.details.async.tradeoffs.co: Eventual consistency makes reasoning about state harder
Debugging distributed event flows is challenging
Message ordering and duplicate handling add complexity
No immediate response to the caller
microservices.details.async.tradeoffs.wh: Best for workflows where services can react independently and immediate consistency is not required. Ideal for high-throughput systems with varying processing speeds.
microservices.details.saga.principles: Distributed transactions are broken into a sequence of local transactions
Each step has a compensating transaction to undo its effects on failure
Saga Orchestrator coordinates the sequence and handles failures
No distributed locks — each service commits locally
Compensations must be idempotent and safe to retry

microservices.details.saga.concepts.definition: Central coordinator that manages the saga workflow: sends commands, waits for replies, triggers compensations on failure.
An operation that semantically undoes a previously committed transaction (e.g., refund payment, release inventory).
Each service commits changes to its own database independently, without a global distributed transaction.
A single local transaction in the saga sequence. Each step has a corresponding compensation action.

microservices.details.saga.tradeoffs.pro: Maintains data consistency across services without distributed locks
Clear workflow visibility through the orchestrator
Each service remains autonomous with local transactions
Failure handling is explicit and well-defined
microservices.details.saga.tradeoffs.con: Complex to implement — each step needs a compensating action
Orchestrator can become a single point of failure
Debugging failed sagas across multiple services is difficult
microservices.details.saga.tradeoffs.whe: Best for business operations that span multiple services and require data consistency (e.g., order fulfillment, booking). Use when two-phase commit is not feasible.
microservices.details.saga-fail.principl: Compensating transactions undo committed steps in reverse order
Each compensation must be idempotent — safe to retry on failure
Orchestrator tracks which steps succeeded to know what to compensate
Compensations are semantic inverses, not rollbacks (e.g., refund vs. undo payment)
The system remains in a consistent state after all compensations complete

microservices.details.saga-fail.concepts.definition: An operation that semantically undoes a previously committed local transaction (e.g., release inventory, cancel payment).
The process of executing compensating transactions in reverse order of the original saga steps to restore consistency.
A persistent record of saga progress. Tracks which steps completed and which compensations are needed on failure.
Unlike database rollback, compensations create new transactions that reverse the business effect (e.g., a refund creates a new credit, not a deletion).

microservices.details.saga-fail.tradeoff: Guarantees eventual consistency even on partial failures
Each service remains autonomous — no distributed locks
Failure handling is explicit and auditable via saga log
Compensations can include business logic (notifications, alerts)
Every step must have a well-defined compensation — doubles implementation effort
Compensations themselves can fail, requiring retry logic
Intermediate inconsistency is visible during compensation execution
Complex to test all failure-and-compensation scenarios
Essential when distributed transactions can fail at any step and you need automated recovery. Design compensations from the start — retrofitting them is extremely difficult.
ddd.name: Domain-Driven Design
ddd.modes.http.label: HTTP Request
ddd.modes.http.desc: HTTP request enters the Bounded Context through the Presentation layer (Controller). UseCase orchestrates domain logic and persists the Aggregate via Repository. Domain Events propagate between contexts through the EventBus.
ddd.modes.console.label: Console
ddd.modes.console.desc: Console command triggers a use case within a Bounded Context. Domain logic stays isolated inside the context boundary, communicating with other contexts via Domain Events.
ddd.modes.message.label: Message
ddd.modes.message.desc: Domain Event from another Bounded Context arrives through the Anti-Corruption Layer, gets translated, and processed within the local context. This demonstrates context mapping.
ddd.modes.context-map.label: Context Map
ddd.modes.context-map.desc: Bird's-eye view of all Bounded Contexts and their strategic relationships: ACL, Shared Kernel, Customer-Supplier, Conformist. Before writing code, define how contexts relate to each other.
ddd.modes.open-host.label: Open Host
ddd.modes.open-host.desc: A context publishes a formal API (Open Host Service) with Published Language (OpenAPI/Protobuf). Multiple downstream contexts consume the same standardized contract instead of point-to-point translations.
ddd.modes.saga.label: Saga ✓
ddd.modes.saga.desc: Saga/Process Manager coordinates a business process spanning multiple Bounded Contexts. Each context owns its local transaction; the Saga orchestrates the overall workflow. All steps succeed: Order → Payment → Shipment → COMPLETED.
ddd.modes.saga-fail.label: Saga ✗
ddd.modes.saga-fail.desc: Payment fails after Order is confirmed. Saga Orchestrator triggers compensating transactions in reverse order: CancelOrder undoes the committed step. State machine reaches COMPENSATED instead of COMPLETED.
ddd.modes.domain-events.label: Domain Events
ddd.modes.domain-events.desc: A Domain Event propagates through the Event Bus to multiple contexts. Each consumer translates the event through its own ACL/Translator into its local Ubiquitous Language, demonstrating eventual consistency.
ddd.details.http.principles: Bounded Contexts define clear ownership boundaries for domain models
Ubiquitous Language ensures developers and domain experts share the same vocabulary
Anti-Corruption Layer protects context boundaries from external model pollution
Aggregates enforce transactional consistency within a boundary

ddd.details.http.concepts.definition: A boundary within which a particular domain model is defined and applicable. Each context has its own Ubiquitous Language.
A cluster of domain objects treated as a unit for data changes. Has a root entity that controls access.
A translation layer that isolates a Bounded Context from external models, preventing foreign concepts from leaking in.
A record of something significant that happened in the domain. Used for cross-context communication.

ddd.details.http.tradeoffs.pros: Rich domain models that closely reflect business reality
Ubiquitous Language bridges communication between devs and domain experts
Bounded Contexts enable team autonomy and independent deployability
Complex domain logic is well organized within aggregates and services
ddd.details.http.tradeoffs.cons: Steep learning curve — requires deep understanding of strategic and tactical patterns
Overkill for simple CRUD applications with little business logic
Significant upfront modeling effort before writing code
Aggregate boundary design is notoriously difficult to get right
ddd.details.http.tradeoffs.whenToUse: Best for complex business domains with many rules, multiple teams working on different subdomains, and evolving requirements where the cost of misunderstanding the domain is high.
ddd.details.console.principles: Console and HTTP share the same domain model within a Bounded Context
Domain Services coordinate logic that spans multiple aggregates
Events propagate state changes without coupling contexts directly
Application layer orchestrates use cases without containing business rules

ddd.details.console.concepts.definition: A stateless service that encapsulates domain logic which does not naturally belong to a single Entity or Value Object.
Orchestrates use case execution, coordinates domain objects, but contains no business logic itself.

ddd.details.console.tradeoffs.pros: Rich domain models that closely reflect business reality
Ubiquitous Language bridges communication between devs and domain experts
Bounded Contexts enable team autonomy and independent deployability
Complex domain logic is well organized within aggregates and services
ddd.details.console.tradeoffs.cons: Steep learning curve — requires deep understanding of strategic and tactical patterns
Overkill for simple CRUD applications with little business logic
Significant upfront modeling effort before writing code
Aggregate boundary design is notoriously difficult to get right
ddd.details.console.tradeoffs.whenToUse: Best for complex business domains with many rules, multiple teams working on different subdomains, and evolving requirements where the cost of misunderstanding the domain is high.
ddd.details.message.principles: Context Mapping defines relationships between Bounded Contexts
ACL translates external events into local domain language
Domain Events are the primary mechanism for cross-context communication
Each context maintains its own model integrity

ddd.details.message.concepts.definition: A visual and conceptual representation of how Bounded Contexts relate to each other (Shared Kernel, Customer-Supplier, Conformist, ACL).
Contexts communicate asynchronously through domain events rather than direct API calls, reducing coupling.

ddd.details.message.tradeoffs.pros: Rich domain models that closely reflect business reality
Ubiquitous Language bridges communication between devs and domain experts
Bounded Contexts enable team autonomy and independent deployability
Complex domain logic is well organized within aggregates and services
ddd.details.message.tradeoffs.cons: Steep learning curve — requires deep understanding of strategic and tactical patterns
Overkill for simple CRUD applications with little business logic
Significant upfront modeling effort before writing code
Aggregate boundary design is notoriously difficult to get right
ddd.details.message.tradeoffs.whenToUse: Best for complex business domains with many rules, multiple teams working on different subdomains, and evolving requirements where the cost of misunderstanding the domain is high.
ddd.details.context-map.principles: Context Map is the first strategic artifact — draw it before writing code
Each relationship type implies a different integration strategy and team dynamics
Power asymmetry between teams determines relationship type (upstream/downstream)
Relationships evolve over time as teams and domains change

ddd.details.context-map.concepts.definition: Downstream context builds a translation layer to protect itself from upstream model changes. Maximum isolation, highest development cost.
Two contexts share a subset of the domain model (code/schema). Changes require agreement from both teams. Tight coupling but reduces duplication.
Upstream (supplier) provides what downstream (customer) needs. Customer can negotiate requirements. Formal interface with versioning.
Downstream context accepts the upstream model as-is without translation. Simplest integration but no protection from upstream changes.
A visual representation of all Bounded Contexts in the system and the relationships between them. The starting point for strategic DDD.

ddd.details.context-map.tradeoffs.pros: Gives a bird's-eye view of the entire system architecture
Makes team relationships and power dynamics explicit
Guides integration strategy decisions before coding
ddd.details.context-map.tradeoffs.cons: Requires organization-wide understanding of domain boundaries
Relationships can be subjective and debatable
Map must be maintained as the system evolves
Does not capture runtime behavior or data flow
ddd.details.context-map.tradeoffs.whenTo: Essential for any multi-team, multi-context system. Draw the Context Map first when starting a new project or onboarding into an existing one.
ddd.details.open-host.principles: Open Host Service provides a single formal API for all consumers
Published Language defines a shared schema (OpenAPI, Protobuf, Avro)
Upstream context owns and versions the contract independently
Consumers are decoupled from the internal domain model of the provider

ddd.details.open-host.concepts.definition: A well-defined, public API that a Bounded Context exposes for consumption. Unlike point-to-point integrations, OHS serves all consumers uniformly.
A shared data format (JSON Schema, Protobuf, Avro) that defines the contract. It is the "lingua franca" between contexts.
The API schema is designed before implementation. Consumers can start integration work in parallel with provider development.

ddd.details.open-host.tradeoffs.pros: Single contract serves multiple consumers — reduces integration effort
Published Language provides clear documentation and versioning
Consumers are insulated from internal model changes
ddd.details.open-host.tradeoffs.cons: Contract evolution requires careful versioning strategy
One-size-fits-all API may not suit every consumer optimally
Upstream team bears the cost of maintaining the public API
Schema changes can have widespread downstream impact
ddd.details.open-host.tradeoffs.whenToUs: When a context has 3+ consumers and you want to avoid building custom integrations for each. Standard approach for platform/core services.
ddd.details.saga.principles: Each Bounded Context owns its local transaction — no distributed transactions
Saga Orchestrator coordinates the overall workflow by sending commands
Each step produces an event that the Saga listens to for state transitions
Compensation actions are defined for each step to handle failures

ddd.details.saga.concepts.definition: A stateful process manager that coordinates a multi-step business process. It sends commands to contexts and reacts to their events.
The reverse operation for a previously completed step. Used to maintain consistency when a later step fails (e.g., refund payment if shipping fails).
The orchestrator tracks its progress through the workflow (e.g., ORDER_CONFIRMED → PAYMENT_COMPLETED → SHIPMENT_CREATED).
The system reaches a consistent state eventually, not immediately. Each local transaction is committed independently.

ddd.details.saga.tradeoffs.pros: Avoids distributed transactions (2PC) which don't scale
Each context remains autonomous with its own database
Clear compensation logic for failure handling
Orchestrator provides single point of workflow visibility
ddd.details.saga.tradeoffs.cons: Complex to implement — requires careful state machine design
Compensation logic can be as complex as the happy path
ddd.details.saga.tradeoffs.whenToUse: When a business process spans 3+ Bounded Contexts and you need consistency without distributed transactions. Common in e-commerce, booking, and financial systems.
ddd.details.saga-fail.principles: Compensating transactions undo committed steps in reverse order
Each compensation must be idempotent — safe to retry on failure
Orchestrator tracks which steps succeeded to know what to compensate
Compensations are semantic inverses, not database rollbacks (e.g., CancelOrder vs. undo)
The system reaches COMPENSATED state — consistent, not broken

ddd.details.saga-fail.concepts.definition: An operation that semantically undoes a previously committed local transaction. CancelOrder reverses CreateOrder, but it is a new command, not a rollback.
Executing compensating transactions in reverse order of the original saga steps. Only committed steps need compensation.
States include COMPENSATING and COMPENSATED in addition to happy path states. The orchestrator transitions to COMPENSATING on any step failure.
Unlike database rollback, compensation creates new domain events (OrderCancelled) that other contexts can react to.

ddd.details.saga-fail.tradeoffs.pros: Guarantees eventual consistency even on partial failures
Each context remains autonomous — no distributed locks needed
Failure handling is explicit and auditable via saga state machine
Compensations produce domain events that other contexts can consume
ddd.details.saga-fail.tradeoffs.cons: Every step must have a well-defined compensation — doubles implementation effort
Compensations themselves can fail, requiring retry and dead-letter strategies
Intermediate inconsistency is visible during compensation execution
State machine complexity grows with each step added to the saga
ddd.details.saga-fail.tradeoffs.whenToUs: Essential when distributed transactions can fail at any step. Design compensations from the start — retrofitting them into an existing saga is extremely difficult.
ddd.details.domain-events.principles: Domain Events represent facts — something that already happened
Each consumer interprets the event in its own Ubiquitous Language via ACL
Event Bus decouples producer from consumers — producer doesn't know who listens
Eventual consistency is the natural outcome of event-driven integration

ddd.details.domain-events.concepts.definition: An immutable record of a significant business occurrence (e.g., OrderPlaced). Named in past tense using domain language.
Each consumer translates the incoming event into its local domain concept. OrderPlaced becomes PaymentRequired in Payment context.
Infrastructure that routes events from producers to consumers. Can be RabbitMQ, Kafka, or any message broker.
The same real-world fact means different things in different contexts. The ACL bridges these language differences.

ddd.details.domain-events.tradeoffs.pros: Maximum decoupling — producer doesn't know its consumers
New consumers can subscribe without changing the producer
Each context maintains its own model integrity through ACL
Natural fit for microservices and distributed systems
ddd.details.domain-events.tradeoffs.cons: Eventual consistency requires careful handling in UIs
Event ordering and idempotency must be explicitly managed
Debugging event chains across contexts is difficult
Event schema evolution needs a versioning strategy
ddd.details.domain-events.tradeoffs.when: When a single business fact triggers reactions in multiple contexts. The go-to pattern for cross-context communication in DDD. Prefer over synchronous calls for loose coupling.
layered.requestLabel.http: Layered: request flows top-down through Presentation → Application → Domain → Infrastructure
layered.requestLabel.console: Layered: CLI command follows the same top-down layer flow as HTTP
layered.requestLabel.message: Layered: async message follows the same top-down layer flow as HTTP and CLI
layered.step.http.0: POST /api/orders hits Controller — entry point of the topmost Presentation layer
layered.step.console.0: app:sync-users invoked — CLI handler parses arguments in Presentation layer
layered.step.message.0: order.created message dequeued — async entry into Presentation layer
clean.requestLabel.http: Clean: request traverses rings inward — Frameworks → Adapters → Use Cases → Entities
clean.requestLabel.console: Clean: CLI enters outermost ring — same Use Cases and Entities serve any delivery mechanism
clean.requestLabel.message: Clean: message consumed at outermost ring — queue driver is a framework detail
clean.step.http.0: GET /api/users/1 arrives at outermost ring — framework is a replaceable detail
clean.step.console.0: migrate:run enters CLI framework — outermost ring, interchangeable detail
clean.step.message.0: user.registered message consumed — queue driver in outermost ring
hexagonal.requestLabel.http: Hexagonal: driving adapter translates HTTP into domain port call
hexagonal.requestLabel.console: Hexagonal: CLI adapter uses the same input port as HTTP
hexagonal.requestLabel.message: Hexagonal: message adapter calls the same input port as HTTP and CLI
hexagonal.step.http.0: PUT /api/products/42 hits HTTP adapter — translates to input port call
hexagonal.step.console.0: product:import invoked — CLI adapter converts arguments into input port call
hexagonal.step.message.0: inventory.updated consumed — message adapter deserializes and calls input port
ddd.requestLabel.http: DDD: request enters Order Bounded Context — isolated from Payment and Shipping
ddd.requestLabel.console: DDD: CLI triggers Bounded Context use case — domain logic stays isolated
ddd.requestLabel.message: DDD: event from Payment Context translated via Anti-Corruption Layer
ddd.requestLabel.open-host: DDD: aggregate event published through Open Host Service to downstream contexts
ddd.requestLabel.saga: DDD Saga: orchestrator coordinates Order, Payment, Shipping as local transactions
ddd.requestLabel.saga-fail: DDD Saga: failure triggers compensating transactions in reverse order
ddd.requestLabel.domain-events: DDD: domain event fans out to all subscribing bounded contexts
ddd.step.http.0: POST /api/orders enters Order Context — context boundary isolates this domain
ddd.step.console.0: order:recalculate enters context — same use case as HTTP, behind context boundary
ddd.step.message.0: payment.completed event arrives — ACL will translate to Order Context language
ddd.step.open-host.0: PlaceOrder use case starts — aggregate will raise event for Open Host Service
ddd.step.saga.0: OrderSaga initializes — state ORDER_PENDING, orchestrator begins coordination
ddd.step.saga-fail.0: OrderSaga initializes — state ORDER_PENDING, compensation will undo on failure
ddd.step.domain-events.0: PlaceOrder receives CreateOrder command — aggregate will raise OrderPlaced event
cqrs.requestLabel.http: CQRS: write side — commands are explicit objects, never direct state mutations
cqrs.requestLabel.console: CQRS: read side — queries read from optimized denormalized projections
cqrs.requestLabel.message: CQRS: event triggers both write-side command and read-side projection update
cqrs.step.http.0: CreateOrderCommand dispatched to write side — explicit command object created
cqrs.step.console.0: GetOrderDetails query dispatched to read side — never touches write model
cqrs.step.message.0: PaymentReceived event arrives on the bus — will update both sides
eda.requestLabel.http: EDA: service publishes event instead of calling downstream services directly
eda.requestLabel.choreography: EDA Choreography: no coordinator — services independently react and emit events
eda.requestLabel.orchestration: EDA Orchestration: central coordinator sends commands and tracks completion
eda.step.http.0: POST /api/orders creates an order — publishes event for interested consumers
eda.step.choreography.0: OrderCreated event emitted — each service will react independently
eda.step.orchestration.0: Order created — Saga Orchestrator takes control of the workflow
eventsourcing.requestLabel.http: Event Sourcing: command produces event appended to immutable stream, not a row update
eventsourcing.requestLabel.console: Event Sourcing: state rebuilt by replaying all events in sequence
eventsourcing.requestLabel.message: Event Sourcing: projections independently transform events into read-optimized views
eventsourcing.step.http.0: AddItem command received — aggregate will emit ItemAdded event
eventsourcing.step.console.0: Rebuild Order #42 from event stream — no stored state exists
eventsourcing.step.message.0: OrderConfirmed event read from store — projections will transform it
microservices.requestLabel.sync: Microservices Sync: client → API Gateway → services (no direct calls)
microservices.requestLabel.async: Microservices Async: services react to events via Message Queue
microservices.requestLabel.saga: Microservices Saga: orchestrator coordinates inventory, payment, confirmation
microservices.requestLabel.saga-fail: Microservices Saga Failure: payment fails, compensating transactions undo inventory
microservices.step.sync.0: POST /api/orders sent to API Gateway — gateway handles auth and routing
microservices.step.async.0: Order created locally — OrderCreated event published to Message Queue
microservices.step.saga.0: POST /api/orders — routed to Saga Orchestrator for distributed coordination
microservices.step.saga-fail.0: POST /api/orders — this request will fail at payment step
mvc.requestLabel.mvc: MVC: Controller updates Model, View observes Model (never updated by Controller directly)
mvc.requestLabel.mvp: MVP: passive View delegates all actions to Presenter via IView interface
mvc.requestLabel.mvvm: MVVM: two-way data binding syncs View and ViewModel automatically
mvc.step.mvc.0: GET /users/42 routed to UserController — Controller will update Model
mvc.step.mvp.0: User clicks 'Save' — passive View delegates to Presenter
mvc.step.mvvm.0: User types in search field — binding propagates to ViewModel
mvc.triad.view.desc.mvc: Displays data to user. Observes Model for changes. Sends user actions to Controller.
mvc.triad.controller.desc: Handles user input. Updates Model. Selects View. Does NOT directly update View content.
mvc.triad.model.desc.mvc: Business logic and data. Notifies observers (View) when state changes. Independent of UI.
mvc.triad.view.desc.mvp: Passive view. Delegates ALL input to Presenter. Has no logic. Implements a View interface.
mvc.triad.presenter.desc: Mediator between View and Model. Contains presentation logic. Explicitly updates View through interface.
mvc.triad.model.desc.mvp: Business data and rules. Presenter reads/writes Model. Model has no knowledge of Presenter or View.
mvc.triad.view.desc.mvvm: Binds to ViewModel properties. Two-way data binding keeps UI in sync. Minimal code-behind.
mvc.triad.viewmodel.desc: Exposes observable properties and commands. View binds to these. Transforms Model data for display.
mvc.triad.model.desc.mvvm: Pure data and business logic. ViewModel reads/writes Model. No knowledge of View or ViewModel.
hexagonal.core.label: Domain Core
eventsourcing.eventbus.label: Event Bus
eda.saga.label: Saga Orchestrator
eda.eventbus.label: Event Bus / Message Broker
microservices.mesh.label: Service Mesh (Istio / Linkerd)
microservices.saga.label: Saga Orchestrator
microservices.queue.label: Message Queue
microservices.tooltip.queue_async: Kafka / RabbitMQ — asynchronous message routing. Decouples producers from consumers for reliable event delivery.
microservices.tooltip.queue_saga: Kafka / RabbitMQ — used by the Saga Orchestrator to publish completion events (e.g., OrderCompleted). Notification Service consumes these events asynchronously.
ddd.acl.label: Anti-Corruption Layer
arch.logtext.716e6895: Aggregate method creates order and raises domain event
arch.logtext.fc366ca3: Aggregate processes command and raises event
arch.logtext.1cb3f15d: Auth OK, route to Order Service
arch.logtext.6bbd8a42: CRM conforms to Order's model as-is — no ACL, no translation. CRM reuses Order's Ubiquitous Language (OrderId, OrderStatus) directly in its Customer and Segment entities
arch.logtext.547ac47: Compensate: Saga sends CancelOrder command (undo Step 1)
arch.logtext.422c13df: Core domain — Order Context is the strategic nucleus of the system
arch.logtext.6c5b69f0: Event Bus distributes OrderPlaced to all subscribers
arch.logtext.e370cd3: Event routed through bus to Saga
arch.logtext.dea2fa6a: Fan-out complete — each consumer processes asynchronously
arch.logtext.725fb0a8: Infrastructure adapter publishes event through OHS
arch.logtext.a225a899: Infrastructure adapter publishes event to external bus
arch.logtext.bf20f09: Open Host Service exposes Published Language (OpenAPI)
arch.logtext.483750a9: Order (downstream customer) negotiates the API contract with Shipping (upstream supplier). Shipping provides delivery capabilities on Order's terms
arch.logtext.64025437: Order and Inventory jointly own the ProductId value object. Both teams must coordinate and agree before changing the shared code
arch.logtext.7002cf49: Order failed \u2014 inventory released, no charge
arch.logtext.4edd0850: Payment builds an Anti-Corruption Layer to protect itself from Order model changes. ACL translates Order concepts into Payment's own Ubiquitous Language
arch.logtext.fd1f9ae3: Payment failed (tx not committed), start compensation
arch.logtext.435658ae: Presenter calls Model: execute business rules
arch.logtext.12e33eb7: Receive request, authenticate via User Service
arch.logtext.5d929e9c: Saga state → COMPENSATED. All compensations done.
arch.logtext.bfd3c5e1: Saga state → COMPENSATING. Starting backward recovery.
arch.logtext.43670b94: Saga state → COMPLETED. All steps successful.
arch.logtext.e0de7ceb: State fully rebuilt: confirmed, paid, 2 items
arch.logtext.1b0abee3: Translates to PaymentRequired in local language
arch.logtext.82261909: Translates to SaleRecorded in local language
arch.logtext.e1c472d2: Translates to ShipmentRequested in local language
arch.logtext.d80ad840: Validate JWT token, return user context
